input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, serving as the **physical twin (PT)** for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Digital Twin system. The vehicle consists of three hardware layers: a **lower-level chassis** (Traxxas Slash 4x4 Premium stripped-down version) responsible for steering and speed control, an **upper-level chassis** hosting autonomy components, and onboard sensors including an **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** microcomputer, **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR**, **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera**, and **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller**. The PT operates under **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** middleware and **NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0** SDK for programming the Jetson Orin Nano, with application modules implemented in **Python 3.8**, **C++**, and **MATLAB/Simulink** for complex systems like point cloud clustering and tracking.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical acting components in the Digital Twin constellation include:
   - **Motor actuation** for speed control via **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** interfacing with the vehicle's motor.
   - **Steering servo actuation** for directional control, integrated into the lower-level chassis.
   These components receive **desired speed commands** and **steering adjustments** from the Digital Twin Entity via the **Actuation Functional Entity (FE)** and execute real-time control signals on the PT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The physical sensing components consist of:
   - **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR** for capturing high-resolution 3D point clouds of the vehicle's environment, enabling object detection and tracking.
   - **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera** for supplementary visual data, including object size, position, and velocity estimation.
   - **Onboard sensors** for ego vehicle state data, including **speed** and **steering angle**, transmitted via ROS2 topics to the Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The interactions from the physical twin to the virtual twin include:
   - **Real-time data streams** from sensors (LiDAR, depth camera, and vehicle state sensors) transmitted via **ROS2** topics to the **Data Pre-Processing FE** in the Physical Twin Entity.
   - **Object tracks** (detected objects in proximity) and **ego vehicle state data** (speed, steering angle) processed by the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** module using **DBSCAN clustering** and **Kalman filtering**.
   - **Data serialization and unit conversion** via the **Data Translation FE** before transmission over **Wi-Fi** to the Digital Twin Entity for further processing and visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The interactions from the virtual twin to the physical twin include:
   - **Desired speed commands** computed by the **ACC Controlling FE** in the Digital Twin Entity, transmitted via **ROS2** and **Wi-Fi** to the **Actuation FE** in the Physical Twin Entity.
   - **Actuation signals** (motor voltage commands and steering adjustments) sent to the **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** and **steering servo** to control the PT's speed and direction.
   - **Emergency braking commands** issued via the **Control Panel** in the User Entity, processed by the **Controlling FE** and transmitted to the PT for immediate intervention.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin system provides the following services:
   - **Data Monitoring**: Real-time visualization of **ego vehicle state** (speed, steering angle), **object tracks**, and **projected driving path** via a **3D Visualization UI**.
   - **Computation Offloading**: Delegation of **ACC system computations** (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation) from the PT to the Digital Twin Entity, reducing the load on the **Jetson Orin Nano**.
   - **Remote Management**: Geographically remote control of the ACC system, including **enable/disable ACC**, **desired speed adjustment**, and **emergency braking**, facilitated by the **Control Panel UI**.
   - **Report Generation**: Creation of **human-readable reports** of object tracks and vehicle state as time-series data via the **Reporting FE** and stored in a **SQL database** for post-processing.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The Digital Twin operates on a **real-time time-scale**, synchronized with the physical twin at a **100 ms clock accuracy** (due to hardware limitations) to ensure low-latency interactions. Key time-scale specifications include:
   - **End-to-end latency** of **≤100 ms** between the PT and Digital Twin Entity, compliant with **3GPP Technical Specification 22.185** for Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication.
   - **Perception cycle frequency** for object detection and tracking (LiDAR and depth camera data processed at **~10-30 Hz** depending on sensor resolution and computational load).
   - **ACC control loop** executed at **~10 Hz** to maintain real-time responsiveness for speed adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin system is implemented in a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** following the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture**, with the following multiplicities:
   - **Three primary entities**:
     1. **Physical Twin Entity** (hosts resource-specific functionality like **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** and **Actuation FE**).
     2. **Digital Twin Entity** (contains **ACC Controlling FE**, **Data Collection FE**, **Reporting FE**, and **Presentation FE**).
     3. **User Entity** (comprising **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** as sub-components of the **User Interface FE**).
   - **Functional Entities (FEs)** are implemented as **individual ROS2 packages**, enabling modular deployment across hardware components (e.g., **Jetson Orin Nano** for PT, cloud/edge for DT).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin system supports **operational and developmental lifecycle stages** of the physical twin, covering:
   - **As-Operated Phase**: Real-time monitoring, remote management, and **predictive maintenance** (e.g., ACC performance optimization, emergency braking).
   - **Design/Validation Phase**: Use of the DT as a **cyber-physical experimentation facility** for testing ACC algorithms, validating compliance with **ISO 15622** (Performance Class I ACC), and simulating **purposeful experiments** for data-driven improvements.
   - **Lifecycle Integration**: Facilitates **digital threads** for vehicle management by maintaining continuous data flow from design to operation via the DT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system includes the following models and data components:
   - **Physical Models**:
     - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Model**: Uses **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** for object detection and **Kalman Filter** for state estimation of detected objects.
     - **ACC Control Model**: Implements **ISO 15622-compliant** control logic for **Performance Class I ACC**, including lead vehicle detection and speed adjustment algorithms.
   - **Data Components**:
     - **Raw Sensor Data**: LiDAR point clouds and depth camera frames transmitted via ROS2 topics.
     - **Processed Data**: Object tracks (size, position, velocity), ego vehicle state (speed, steering angle), and **SQL database** for storing historical data.
     - **Visualization Data**: 3D-rendered object tracks, vehicle state, and driving path for the **User Interface FE** (using **ROS2 + 3D visualization libraries**).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin system leverages the following tools and enablers:
   - **Middleware**: **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** for inter-node communication, node lifecycle management, and hardware abstraction.
   - **Hardware Platforms**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** (for PT onboard computing) with **Jetpack 5.0 SDK**.
     - **Cloud/Edge Servers** (for Digital Twin Entity, offloading computation).
   - **Development Tools**:
     - **Python 3.8** (rapid prototyping), **C++** (ROS2 node lifecycle), **MATLAB/Simulink** (complex systems like point cloud processing).
     - **OPC UA (IEC 62541)** for potential future data translation standardization.
   - **Visualization**: **3D Visualization UI** built on ROS2-compatible libraries for real-time rendering of object tracks and vehicle state.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation is orchestrated as a **distributed, entity-based system** with the following components and interactions:
   - **Physical Twin (PT)**: Hosts **sensors (LiDAR, depth camera)**, **actuators (motor, steering servo)**, and **ROS2 nodes** for data acquisition and pre-processing.
   - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Executes **ACC logic**, **data collection**, **reporting**, and **visualization pre-processing** on a separate hardware (cloud/edge), connected via **Wi-Fi**.
   - **User Entity (UE)**: Provides **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** interfaces for remote monitoring and management, deployed on a **separate UI server**.
   - **Data Flow**:
     1. **PT → DTE**: Sensor data (ROS2 topics) → **Data Translation FE** → **Wi-Fi** → **Digital Twin Entity**.
     2. **DTE → PT**: Control commands (ROS2 topics) → **Actuation FE** → **Wi-Fi** → **Physical Twin Entity**.
     3. **DTE → UE**: Processed data → **Presentation FE** → **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** for user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin engineering process follows a **requirement-driven, iterative development** approach with the following milestones:
   - **Requirements Definition**: Derived from **case-based generalization** and automotive expertise, including **R1-R8** (e.g., **100 ms latency**, **ROS2 standardization**, **ISO 15622 compliance**).
   - **Architectural Design**: Adaption of **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture**, with deviations such as introducing a **Physical Twin Entity** and splitting the **User Interface FE** into **Visualization** and **Control Panel**.
   - **Implementation**: Each **Functional Entity (FE)** implemented as a **ROS2 package**, deployed on **Jetson Orin Nano (PT)** or **cloud/edge (DTE)**.
   - **Quality Assurance**: Validation of **end-to-end latency (<100 ms)**, **clock synchronization (±100 ms)**, and **ACC compliance (ISO 15622)**.
   - **Evolution**: Future upgrades include **AI simulation integration** (via **User Entity**) and **purposeful experimentation** for data-driven improvements.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system ensures fidelity and validity through:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: **100 ms clock accuracy** and **<100 ms latency** to maintain temporal consistency between PT and DT.
   - **Sensor Calibration**: **LiDAR and depth camera** data validated via **DBSCAN clustering** and **Kalman filtering** for accurate object detection and tracking.
   - **Control Validation**: **ACC logic** validated against **ISO 15622 (Performance Class I)** standards, ensuring compliance with safety-critical requirements.
   - **Data Integrity**: **ROS2** ensures reliable message passing, while **SQL database** storage guarantees historical data consistency for reporting.
   - **Uncertainty Handling**: **Kalman Filter** accounts for sensor noise and environmental variations in object state estimation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The Digital Twin system employs the following technical connections:
   - **Internal Communication**:
     - **ROS2** for intra-entity communication (e.g., **PT Entity** nodes ↔ **DTE** nodes on the same hardware).
     - **Wi-Fi (UDP/TCP)** for inter-entity communication between **PT** and **DTE**, with **<100 ms end-to-end latency**.
   - **Data Protocols**:
     - **ROS2 topics** for sensor data (LiDAR, depth camera) and control commands (motor voltage, steering adjustments).
     - **Data Translation FE** serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions (e.g., **m/s → motor PWM**).
   - **Network Architecture**: **Distributed system** with **PT (Jetson Orin Nano)** and **DTE (cloud/edge)** connected via **Wi-Fi**, adhering to **3GPP 22.185 V2V communication standards**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The Digital Twin system is hosted and deployed across:
   - **Physical Twin (PT)**: Deployed on the **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle**, running **ROS2** and **Jetpack 5.0** on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano**.
   - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Hosted on **cloud/edge servers** (separate from PT) to offload computation, with **ROS2** nodes managing data processing, ACC logic, and reporting.
   - **User Entity (UE)**: Deployed on a **separate UI server** (local/cloud) for **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel**, accessible via **web-based or desktop applications**.
   - **Data Storage**: **SQL database** hosted on the **DTE** or a **dedicated database server** for storing processed data and reports.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin provides indirect insights and decision-making outputs, including:
   - **Performance Optimization**: Real-time **fuel economy** and **driving performance** insights via **spark ignition DT integration** (e.g., Ramtilak et al. [23]).
   - **Predictive Maintenance**: Early detection of **brake system failures** or **motor degradation** through **state estimation** and **historical data analysis**.
   - **Driver Assistance**: **Transparent human-machine interfaces** (e.g., **3D Visualization UI**) for **trust-building** and **decision support** in ADAS systems.
   - **Data-Driven Improvements**: **Report generation** and **purposeful experimentation** enable iterative refinement of **ACC algorithms** and **sensor calibration**.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates horizontally with:
   - **External Simulators**: Potential integration with **CREO Simulate** (as in Rajesh et al. [25]) for **high-fidelity motor simulations** or **3D CAD tools** (e.g., **SolidWorks, CATIA**) for **design-phase validation**.
   - **Fleet Management Systems**: Cloud-based **fleet coordination** (e.g., **cloud-hosted DTs** for multiple vehicles) for **remote diagnostics** and **over-the-air updates**.
   - **AI/ML Platforms**: Future integration with **AI simulation** (Liu and David [15]) via the **User Entity** for **training AI agents** on simulated data.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
Data ownership and privacy considerations in the system include:
   - **Data Ownership**: Primarily owned by the **vehicle manufacturer/operator**, with **PT sensor data** (e.g., LiDAR, depth camera) considered proprietary.
   - **Privacy Measures**:
     - **Anonymization** of object tracks and vehicle state data for **public-facing visualizations** (e.g., **3D Visualization UI**).
     - **Access Control**: **Role-based authentication** (operators vs. developers) for the **Control Panel UI**, restricting remote management to authorized personnel.
     - **Data Encryption**: **Wi-Fi communication** between PT and DTE secured via **TLS/SSL** or **V2V security protocols** (e.g., **3GPP 33.210** for vehicular networks).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the following standards:
   - **ISO 23247**: **Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework (Part 2: Reference Architecture)** as the foundational architecture, adapted for automotive use with deviations (e.g., **Physical Twin Entity**, split **User Interface FE**).
   - **ISO 15622**: **Road Vehicles – Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Systems** for **Performance Class I ACC** compliance.
   - **IEC 62541 (OPC UA)**: Potential future use for **data translation standardization** between entities.
   - **3GPP 22.185**: **Service Requirements for V2V Communication**, defining **<100 ms latency** and **clock synchronization (±100 ms)**.
   - **ROS2**: **Middleware standard** for inter-node communication and hardware abstraction.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system incorporates security and safety measures to mitigate risks:
   - **Safety**:
     - **Real-Time Constraints**: **<100 ms latency** and **100 ms clock accuracy** ensure **deterministic control** for ACC operations.
     - **Emergency Braking**: **Hardware-level intervention** via **Control Panel UI** for immediate PT response.
     - **ISO 15622 Compliance**: **Performance Class I ACC** validated to ensure **safety-critical functionality**.
   - **Security**:
     - **Network Security**: **Wi-Fi communication** secured with **TLS/SSL** or **V2V security protocols** (e.g., **3GPP 33.210**) to prevent **cyber-physical attacks**.
     - **Access Control**: **Role-based authentication** for **remote management** (operators vs. developers) to prevent unauthorized PT actuation.
     - **Data Integrity**: **ROS2** ensures **reliable message passing**, while **SQL database** storage includes **checksums** for data consistency.
   - **Ethical Considerations**: **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)** design (David and Bork [30]) ensures **operator oversight** for critical decisions, aligning with **Fully Autonomous Digital Twin** taxonomy.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures: A Case Study

� �
Stefan Ramdhan, Winnie Trandinh [, Istvan David, Vera Pantelic, Mark Lawford](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4870-8433)
McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert), Hamilton, Canada
McMaster University, Hamilton, Canada



_**Abstract**_ **—Digital twin (DT) technology has become of interest**
**in the automotive industry. There is a growing need for smarter**
**services that utilize the unique capabilities of DTs, ranging from**
**computer-aided remote control to cloud-based fleet coordination.**
**Developing such services starts with the software architecture.**
**However, the scarcity of DT architectural guidelines poses a**
**challenge for engineering automotive DTs. Currently, the only**
**DT architectural standard is the one defined in ISO 23247.**
**Though not developed for automotive systems, it is one of the**
**few feasible starting points for automotive DTs. In this work, we**
**investigate the suitability of the ISO 23247 reference architecture**
**for developing automotive DTs. Through the case study of**
**developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10** **[th]** **-scale**
**autonomous vehicle, we identify some strengths and limitations**
**of the reference architecture and begin distilling future directions**
**for researchers, practitioners, and standard developers.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—architecture, automotive, case study, ISO 23247**


I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs


�
S. Ramdhan and W. Trandinh contributed to the paper equally.



outside manufacturing are currently architected through standards that offer solutions to sub-problems of DT engineering.
Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)


(UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data
Translation FE, or implement a custom communication layer.
Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_


In the automotive context, the _ego vehicle_ refers to the
vehicle of interest hosting hardware and software that enable
autonomous driving. Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras,
and other sensors are fused in real time to detect objects in the
ego vehicle’s environment. Each object is typically assigned
an ID and is characterized by its state estimate—e.g., size,
position, velocity—which is updated every perception cycle,
and is called an _object track_ . Using these tracks, ACC can
be developed, which is an advanced driver assistance system
(ADAS) that adjusts the ego vehicle’s speed to maintain a
safe following distance behind the vehicle in its driving path,
called the _lead vehicle_ . The driver can set a _desired speed_ to
be maintained when no lead vehicle is present.
DTs can also be used to streamline the development of
vehicles and ADAS systems, or improve their operation [19].
During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often
used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities, thanks to
their advanced simulator components and the tight coupling



between the virtual and physical environments [20]. This is
often achieved in combination with 3D virtual technology, e.g.,
3D CAD design [21]. DTs are also used to validate compliance
with requirements [22]. During vehicle operation, DTs are
used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring
purposes. Ramtilak et al. [23] use a DT of spark ignition
to improve fuel economy and driving performance. Magargle
et al. [24] use DTs to collect vehicle data and analyze
vehicle performance to inform drivers. Rajesh et al. [25]
implement predictive maintenance of a brake system using a
DT, integrating a state-of-the-art simulator, CREO Simulate,
into it. Venkatesan et al. [26] implement a DT for health
monitoring and prognosis of a permanent magnet synchronous
motor. DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management
tool in support of digital threads of vehicles, establishing
a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the
lifecycle [27]. Additional insights into the role and applications
of DTs in automotive are available in the surveys of Deng et al.

[9] and Schwarz and Wang [28].


III. T HE A DAPTIVE C RUISE C ONTROL C ASE S TUDY


The goal of our work is to investigate the fitness of the ISO
23247 RA [10] for developing automotive DTs. To achieve
this goal, we choose the case study empirical genre, which
is “ _an empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary_
_phenomenon (the case) in depth and within its real-world_
_context, especially when the boundaries between phenomenon_
_and context are unclear_ ” [29]. As such, case studies provide
particularly detailed and credible evidence of the investigated
phenomenon. Specifically, we conduct an evaluative case
study by developing the DT for the ACC of a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, then assess the benefits and limitations
of the ISO 23247 architecture in a real-world setting.


_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle
(Fig. 1), built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle [3] by retrofitting
it with autonomy components. In the retrofitting, we follow the
process provided by the open-source RoboRacer project [31].
As shown in Fig. 1b, the physical architecture consists of three
layers. The _lower level chassis_ is responsible for controlling
the steering and the speed of the vehicle. It is a strippeddown version of the off-the-shelf Traxxas Slash 4x4 Premium

vehicle. The _upper level chassis_ is mounted on top of the
lower level chassis to provide a physical platform for mounting
the technology required for enabling autonomous control and
digital twinning. The list of these autonomy components
include an _NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna_ and _NVIDIA Jetson_

_Orin Nano_ microcomputer, and onboard sensors including a


3 [https://traxxas.com/](https://traxxas.com/)



_Hokuyo UST-10LX_ LiDAR, an _Intel Deepsense D435i_ Depth
Camera, and a _Vedder Electronic Speed Controller_ .
We use _NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0_ as the Software Development
Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano, and _Robot_
_Operating System 2_ (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate
application modules. Application modules are implemented in
Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle
management), and MATLAB/Simulink (for more complex
systems, e.g., point cloud clustering and tracking).


IV. E NGINEERING A DT FOR A DAPTIVE C RUISE C ONTROL


We now provide a detailed exposition of the case study,
starting from the definition of DTS requirements (Sec. IV-A).
We then elaborate on the architectural design (Sec. IV-B) and
the implementation of DTS services (Sec. IV-C).


_A. DTS Requirements_


To drive its development, we first define system requirements for the DTS. We rely on a case-based generalization [33] from real projects – an approach that is particularly
effective when constructing middle-range theories that balance
generality with practicality, such as the ones in engineering
sciences. We draw on our expertise with automotive systems
and our ongoing projects in the area as we define the following
representative requirements for the DT.


**R1. The DTS shall use a standardized communication**

**interface between all components of the system.**
Ensures alignment with good system design principles,
such as modularity and loose coupling, to hide internal
complexities of each component.


**R2. The DTS shall collect real-time data about the ego**
**vehicle’s state.** The DTS shall collect the following ACCrelevant data in real-time:


   - ego vehicle’s speed;

   - ego vehicle’s steering angle;

   - detected objects in the vehicle’s proximity.


**R3. The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a**
**device external to the vehicle.** Due to large quantities


TABLE I: SW Components and corresponding FEs


**SW Component** **Description** **ISO 23247 FE**


**Physical Twin Entity**
Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter. Data Pre-Processing FE
Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE


**Digital Twin Entity**
Visualization Pre-Processing Converts data into a format that the 3D Visualization can process. Presentation FE
Data Collection Collects object tracks and ego vehicle state. Data Collecting FE
Report Generation Generates human-readable reports of object tracks and vehicle state as a timeseries. Reporting FE
Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database. _N/A_
ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed. Controlling FE


**User Entity**
3D Visualization Presents 3D object tracks, vehicle state information, and projected driving path. User Interface FE
Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands. User Interface FE


**Cross-System Entity**
Data Translation Translation of exchanged data between entities and FEs. Data Translation FE



of real-time data and limited storage on the vehicle, data
must be stored externally to enable data monitoring.


**R4. The DTS shall report real-time ego vehicle informa-**
**tion through a UI.** Drivers are more likely to trust ADAS
systems with transparent human-machine interfaces that
report relevant information of the ADAS decision-making
process [34]. Thus, the UI shall display the following
information about the ego vehicle in real-time:

   - ego vehicle’s speed;

   - ego vehicle’s steering angle;

   - tracks surrounding the ego vehicle;

   - projected driving path of the ego vehicle.


**R5. The DTS shall include a user interface to remotely**
**manage the ACC sub-system.** To enable remote management, the UI implements the following commands:

   - enable and disable ACC;

   - modify the desired speed of ACC;

   - perform emergency braking of the ego vehicle.


**R6. The DTS shall actuate the ego vehicle in response**
**to user commands and ACC control.** The system
shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622 [35].
The basic control strategy and functionality, described in

[35, Sec 6.1–6.2] shall be implemented for performance
class I ACC (no road curvature capabilities), while the
remaining requirements are not considered due to ACC
being implemented on a 1/10 [th] -scale vehicle.


**R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most**
**100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.** To enable
computation offloading for a real-time system, low endto-end latency is a must. This figure comes from the 3rd
Generation Partnership Plan (3GPP) Technical Specification, 22.185 [36], a specification of service requirements
for Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication.


**R8. Components within the DTS shall have clocks syn-**
**chronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.** Clock synchronization is especially critical in distributed systems
for the purposes of event ordering, data integrity, and



coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_C. Implementation of Services_


Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of
multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II.
All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation
FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and
performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.

ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over
